metaDescription: Stink bug control in NYC. Prevent stink bug invasions before they start. Licensed exterminators. Free inspection. Call or text now.
h1: Stink Bug Control in NYC — Keep Them Out Before They Overwinter Inside
heroParagraph: Brown marmorated stink bugs (BMSB) are an invasive species that arrived in the northeastern United States in the late 1990s and have become a significant fall nuisance pest throughout the NYC metro area, including all five boroughs, New Jersey, and Westchester. They don't bite, don't sting, don't breed indoors, and don't cause structural damage — but they aggregate in enormous numbers in wall voids, attics, and around window frames as they seek overwintering sites in fall, and they release a pungent, persistent odor when disturbed or crushed that has given them a deserved reputation.
bodyParagraphs: The stink bug problem is almost entirely a fall entry and winter aggregation problem. Beginning in September, BMSB move from agricultural and wooded areas toward structures they can overwinter in, entering through window frame gaps, soffit openings, weep holes in brick, and any exterior gap they can fit through (they need about 1/4 inch). Once inside, they congregate in attic spaces, wall voids, and around windows in a dormant state. In spring, they wake up and attempt to exit — which often means they exit into the living space rather than back outdoors.

Prevention is the most effective strategy: sealing exterior gaps in late August before migration begins is more effective than treating a population already inside a wall void. Interior treatment of overwintering populations is possible but challenging since the insects are in a dormant state and contact with insecticide is limited in wall void locations.
signs: Shield-shaped, mottled brown bugs (about 3/4 inch) on exterior siding, screens, or window frames in September–October — gathering to enter
Stink bugs appearing inside from around window frames, light fixtures, or wall outlets — exiting in spring from wall void overwintering sites
Distinctive cilantro-like or pungent odor when bugs are disturbed or crushed
Large aggregations on sunny south and west-facing walls in fall
Dead stink bugs in window sills, light fixtures, or along wall/floor junction in spring
treatmentApproach: Stink bug control uses a two-phase approach: exterior perimeter treatment applied to siding, window frames, soffit areas, and other aggregation surfaces in September before peak migration; and crack-and-crevice sealing of all identified entry points including weep holes, utility penetrations, and window frame gaps. Interior treatment is applied in attic spaces and around window frames to address any population already inside. We prioritize exterior prevention over interior treatment — contact insecticide applied to the exterior intercepts stink bugs before they enter more effectively than treating an overwintering population hidden in wall voids.

faqs.q: Can stink bugs harm me or my pets?
faqs.a: No. Stink bugs don't bite, sting, or carry disease. The only issue is the odor — crushing them releases a strong, persistent smell. Dispose of them by flushing, dropping into soapy water, or vacuuming (empty the vacuum outside immediately). Never crush them.

faqs.q: Will they breed in my house?
faqs.a: No. Stink bugs do not breed indoors. They overwinter in a dormant state and leave in spring to breed on outdoor host plants. The indoor population is purely the overwintering aggregation — it won't grow over the winter.

faqs.q: When should I treat for stink bugs?
faqs.a: The optimal window is late August through September, before the fall migration into structures begins. Exterior perimeter treatment at this time prevents entry more effectively than any interior treatment applied after they've already entered wall voids.

faqs.q: How much does stink bug control cost in NYC?
faqs.a: Stink bug treatment starts at $350–$550. Written estimate provided before starting.

faqs.q: Are stink bugs harmful to people or pets?
faqs.a: No — stink bugs are not harmful to humans or pets. They don't bite, sting, or transmit disease. The only real threat is the odor they release when crushed or disturbed, which can linger for hours. The concern with stink bugs is scale: they overwinter in large aggregations, meaning what starts as a few bugs can become hundreds or thousands in wall voids, attics, and window frames. At that scale, the odor becomes a genuine livability problem.

faqs.q: Why are there so many stink bugs in NYC all of a sudden?
faqs.a: Brown marmorated stink bugs are an invasive species that arrived in the U.S. in the late 1990s and have steadily expanded northward. NYC and the surrounding metro area have seen significant increases over the past decade. With no native predators to control the population, and an abundance of host plants in parks and green spaces, populations have grown substantially. Fall aggravation is highest as bugs migrate toward warm structures.
